title: Correlation enhanced resistance hysteresis near half filling in MoS2/WSe2 heterobilayer
authors: Yong Chen, Weikang Zhang, Meizhen Huang, Shengling Xiang, Zishu Zhou, Yaqi Ma, Chenxuan Lou, Haoxi Ji, Aoqian Zhang, Yifei Jin, Liheng An, Zefei Wu, Chun Cheng, Ning Wang
abstract: Ferroelectricity, typically arising from ionic displacements in noncentrosymmetric lattices, enabling applications in memory devices and sensors. Recent advances in two-dimensional materials and van der Waals heterostructures have revealed novel ferroelectric phenomena, including sliding ferroelectricity and correlation-driven ferroelectricity in moire superlattices. In this work, we fabricate and study a MoS2/WSe2 moire superlattice device exhibiting a high field-effect mobility of 17,650 $cm^2V^{-1}s^{-1}$. Electrical transport measurements reveal correlated insulating states accompanied by a prominent and reproducible resistance hysteresis near half filling. Temperature and displacement field dependence further confirms the correlation-enhanced nature of the hysteresis. Our analysis suggests that displacement field-induced metal-to-insulator transition at correlated insulating state coupled with interfacial dipoles enables the observed resistance hysteresis. These results establish correlation enhanced resistance hysteresis near half filling in a MoS2/WSe2 heterobilayer, offering opportunities for exploring emergent quantum phases and device functionalities.
summary: The authors report observing robust, history-dependent resistance hysteresis in a $	ext{MoS}_2/	ext{WSe}_2$ moiré superlattice near half filling. They argue that this effect is enhanced by the coupling between ferroelectric polarization switching and the correlated insulating state of the material. The findings establish a new route for controlling quantum phases using electric fields in 2D heterostructures.
main_problem: The study investigates the origin of a prominent, reproducible resistance hysteresis observed near half-filling in a $	ext{MoS}_2/	ext{WSe}_2$ moiré superlattice heterobilayer.
main_result:
The hysteresis is established to be correlation-enhanced, arising from an intrinsic coupling between the correlated insulating state and the ferroelectric switching mechanism. This effect is strongly dependent on filling factor ($
u=1$) and diminishes with increasing external electric field ($D$).
method: Electrical transport measurements were performed using a four-probe setup on Hall-bar geometry, sweeping gate voltages to control carrier density and applying an out-of-plane displacement field.
model_or_system: The physical system is a $	ext{MoS}_2/	ext{WSe}_2$ moiré superlattice heterobilayer exhibiting ferroelectricity due to interfacial dipoles. The physics involves correlated insulating states, modeled conceptually by concepts related to the Hubbard model, whose gap size is modulated by external fields and polarization switching.
key_observables: Resistance hysteresis ($\Delta R/R_{	ext{for}}$), temperature dependence of hysteresis (decaying exponentially), field-effect mobility ($\mu_{FE}$), and the insulating energy gap ($\Delta$).
important_parameters:
Half-filling ($
u=1$), critical temperatures ($T_c \approx 4.4 	ext{ K}$), high mobility ($\sim 17,650 	ext{ cm}^2	ext{V}^{-1}	ext{s}^{-1}$), and the external displacement field $D$.
main_assumptions: The hysteresis is attributed to a coupling between ferroelectric polarization switching (modulating the effective field $D_{	ext{eff}}$) and the correlated electronic state undergoing metal-to-insulator transitions.
figures_summary: Figures show high mobility measurements, clear resistance hysteresis loops peaking at half-filling, temperature dependence showing insulating behavior below 8 K, and quantitative analysis mapping the strength of hysteresis vs. displacement field.
paper_structure:
The paper systematically presents transport measurements demonstrating the hysteresis, analyzes its dependence on filling factor ($
u=1$ peak), quantifies the thermal activation gap in the correlated state, and finally links this behavior to the interplay between ferroelectric switching and electronic correlation effects.
why_interesting: This work bridges concepts from strongly correlated electron physics (Hubbard models) with emergent phenomena in 2D van der Waals heterostructures (ferroelectricity), providing a platform for studying field-tunable quantum phases.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
